sections.title: Market Context
sections.content:
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| NAICS Code | 518210 |
| FP Fit | adjacent |
| Architecture Distance | 3 — MEDIUM |
| Market Priority | MEDIUM |
| Türwächter fit in VN | L6d Physical Security & Access Control → L5 Emergency Exit Door Monitoring → L4[1] Door Guardian Alarm Unit |
| L6d activation | Universal — activates across all DC output types (colocation, hyperscale, edge, managed hosting) |
| Competitive baseline | Wired electronic exit alarm + alarm-panel-integrated (~50%); **wireless IoT door alarm guard (~30%)** ← Türwächter category; mechanical-only (~20%) |

---

sections.title: Step 08 — Constraint × Market Assessment
sections.content:
| # | Constraint | Type | is_absolute | Verdict | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | Emergency Escape Non-Impedance | regulatory | false | **none** | Data centers have NFPA 101 / EN 179 fire-code mandated emergency exits. Product's single-motion release already satisfies both EU and US single-motion egress requirements. No additional operational steps introduced. |
| C2 | Mechanical Alarm Independence (Power Fail) | regulatory | true | **none** | DC emergency exits are precisely the context where power-independent operation is most critical — power events (UPS switchover, generator fail, planned maintenance) are routine. The mechanical-primary design is an advantage, not a constraint. |
| C3 | Wireless Range Limitation | physical | false | **mitigable** | Hyperscale DCs (10,000–100,000 m²) and dense RF environments (metallic racks, Faraday-cage construction, server hall shielding) attenuate 868 MHz signal. Multiple IP Gateways / Protocol Bridges required per zone; each gateway covers one security zone (~30 m). |
| C4 | IoT Battery Life Floor | operational | false | **none** | DC emergency exits are genuine emergency-only access points with very low event frequency. Battery life easily exceeds 2-year target. CMMS-driven predictive battery replacement (flagged via battery status events from IoT module) fits standard DC facilities maintenance workflow. |
| C5 | Retrofit-Only Surface Mount | physical | false | **none** | DC emergency exits are located in stairwells, service corridors, and perimeter walls — not in security-critical server halls where flush-mount hardware is required. Surface mount is acceptable in all standard DC emergency exit locations. |
| C6 | EasyWave Protocol Lock-In | operational | false | **mitigable** | Data centers integrate door events into enterprise PACS (Honeywell, Lenel) and SIEM (Splunk, QRadar) for SOC 2 CC6.4 and ISO 27001 A.7.2 audit evidence. EasyWave has zero native interoperability with REST, SNMP, or BACnet-IP. Requires IP Gateway / Protocol Bridge per DC zone. |
| C7 | IoT Price Premium | economic | false | **none** | DC operators are premium buyers with large security budgets. Per-door cost of ~€730 is negligible vs. total DC infrastructure spend. Automated SOC 2 / ISO 27001 audit log generation delivers measurable ROI by reducing compliance labor. |
| C8 | GDPR Monitoring Data Compliance | regulatory | false | **mitigable** | EU data centers must include door-event logging in their DPIA for employee physical access monitoring. DPA with monitoring SaaS provider required. DC operators are compliance-sophisticated with existing GRC platforms — this is an extension of their current program, not a new capability requirement. |
| C9 | IP Protection Boundary | environmental | false | **none** | DC server halls operate at 18–27°C, 40–60% humidity (ASHRAE A1/A2 guidelines), climate-controlled, dust-managed. No washdown. Standard IP class is sufficient for all indoor DC emergency exit locations. |
| C10 | EN 13637 / EltVTR Electrical Compliance | regulatory | false | **mitigable** | Data centers combine emergency exits with electromagnetic door holders, biometric access control, and mantrap interlocks. Combined configurations require EN 13637 system-level certification. System integrator (not GfS) is responsible for combined-system certification; GfS supplies Türwächter compliance documentation as a module. |

---

sections.title: Mitigation Details
sections.content:
| Constraint | Mitigation Actions | Cost | Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| C3 Wireless Range | (1) Deploy IP Gateway / Protocol Bridge per DC security zone; (2) Add 868 MHz repeater per 30 m coverage extension within zones; (3) Map emergency exits per zone during site survey and pre-size gateway count | medium | 2–4 months |
| C6 Protocol Lock-In | (1) Deploy GfS IP Gateway / Protocol Bridge translating EasyWave events to REST/SNMP/BACnet-IP; (2) Configure PACS integration for door-event ingestion; (3) Configure SIEM (Splunk/QRadar) alert rule for unauthorized door events mapped to SOC 2 CC6.4 control | medium | 1–3 months |
| C8 GDPR | (1) Add door-event monitoring to existing DPIA; (2) Execute DPA with GfS or monitoring SaaS provider; (3) Implement data minimization — log event type + timestamp only, no personal identifiers | low | 1–2 months |
| C10 EN 13637 | (1) System integrator certifies combined-system configuration (Türwächter + EM-locks + biometric PACS); (2) GfS provides module-level EN 13637 compliance declaration; (3) Include in DC commissioning documentation package | medium | 3–6 months |

---

sections.title: Constraint Cluster Analysis
sections.content:
**Primary risk cluster for data centers:** C3 (RF range) + C6 (protocol lock-in) — both address the same root cause: integrating an RF-based proprietary device into a standards-based, IP-networked DC security infrastructure. Both are resolved by the same mitigation artifact: **IP Gateway / Protocol Bridge** (already identified as L4 in VN_data_centers.md L6d decomposition).

**Secondary cluster:** C8 (GDPR) + C10 (EN 13637) — compliance documentation requirements that DC operators are already equipped to handle through their GRC platforms and system integrator relationships.

**No knockouts.** NAICS 518210 survives all constraint checks.

---

sections.title: Verdict
sections.content:
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Market status** | **SURVIVING** |
| Knockout constraints | 0 |
| Mitigable constraints | 4 (C3, C6, C8, C10) |
| None / irrelevant | 6 (C1, C2, C4, C5, C7, C9) |
| Mitigation cost ceiling | medium (C3, C6, C10 each medium; C8 low) |
| Mitigation time ceiling | 3–6 months (C10 system certification is the long pole) |
| C6 note | The IP Gateway / Protocol Bridge that resolves C6 is already a named L4 node in the VN. No new infrastructure concept required. |

---

rows.Value: L6d Physical Security & Access Control → L5 Emergency Exit Door Monitoring → L4[1] Door Guardian Alarm Unit
Universal — activates across all DC output types (colocation, hyperscale, edge, managed hosting)
Wired electronic exit alarm + alarm-panel-integrated (~50%); **wireless IoT door alarm guard (~30%)** ← Türwächter category; mechanical-only (~20%)

rows.Rationale: Data centers have NFPA 101 / EN 179 fire-code mandated emergency exits. Product's single-motion release already satisfies both EU and US single-motion egress requirements. No additional operational steps introduced.

DC emergency exits are precisely the context where power-independent operation is most critical — power events (UPS switchover, generator fail, planned maintenance) are routine. The mechanical-primary design is an advantage, not a constraint.

Hyperscale DCs (10,000–100,000 m²) and dense RF environments (metallic racks, Faraday-cage construction, server hall shielding) attenuate 868 MHz signal. Multiple IP Gateways / Protocol Bridges required per zone; each gateway covers one security zone (~30 m).

DC emergency exits are genuine emergency-only access points with very low event frequency. Battery life easily exceeds 2-year target. CMMS-driven predictive battery replacement (flagged via battery status events from IoT module) fits standard DC facilities maintenance workflow.

DC emergency exits are located in stairwells, service corridors, and perimeter walls — not in security-critical server halls where flush-mount hardware is required. Surface mount is acceptable in all standard DC emergency exit locations.

Data centers integrate door events into enterprise PACS (Honeywell, Lenel) and SIEM (Splunk, QRadar) for SOC 2 CC6.4 and ISO 27001 A.7.2 audit evidence. EasyWave has zero native interoperability with REST, SNMP, or BACnet-IP. Requires IP Gateway / Protocol Bridge per DC zone.

DC operators are premium buyers with large security budgets. Per-door cost of ~€730 is negligible vs. total DC infrastructure spend. Automated SOC 2 / ISO 27001 audit log generation delivers measurable ROI by reducing compliance labor.

EU data centers must include door-event logging in their DPIA for employee physical access monitoring. DPA with monitoring SaaS provider required. DC operators are compliance-sophisticated with existing GRC platforms — this is an extension of their current program, not a new capability requirement.

DC server halls operate at 18–27°C, 40–60% humidity (ASHRAE A1/A2 guidelines), climate-controlled, dust-managed. No washdown. Standard IP class is sufficient for all indoor DC emergency exit locations.

Data centers combine emergency exits with electromagnetic door holders, biometric access control, and mantrap interlocks. Combined configurations require EN 13637 system-level certification. System integrator (not GfS) is responsible for combined-system certification; GfS supplies Türwächter compliance documentation as a module.

rows.Mitigation Actions: (1) Deploy IP Gateway / Protocol Bridge per DC security zone; (2) Add 868 MHz repeater per 30 m coverage extension within zones; (3) Map emergency exits per zone during site survey and pre-size gateway count

(1) Deploy GfS IP Gateway / Protocol Bridge translating EasyWave events to REST/SNMP/BACnet-IP; (2) Configure PACS integration for door-event ingestion; (3) Configure SIEM (Splunk/QRadar) alert rule for unauthorized door events mapped to SOC 2 CC6.4 control
(1) Add door-event monitoring to existing DPIA; (2) Execute DPA with GfS or monitoring SaaS provider; (3) Implement data minimization — log event type + timestamp only, no personal identifiers

(1) System integrator certifies combined-system configuration (Türwächter + EM-locks + biometric PACS); (2) GfS provides module-level EN 13637 compliance declaration; (3) Include in DC commissioning documentation package

rows.Value: medium (C3, C6, C10 each medium; C8 low)
3–6 months (C10 system certification is the long pole)
The IP Gateway / Protocol Bridge that resolves C6 is already a named L4 node in the VN. No new infrastructure concept required.
